Title: Photocatalysis as the ‘master switch’ of photomorphogenesis in early plant development

Common_Genes.Applied_Species: Plant (angiosperms; review includes Arabidopsis, barley, cyanobacteria examples)
Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Hordeum vulgare; broader Viridiplantae context
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: LPOR is a prime engineering target to improve chlorophyll establishment, seedling greening and de-etiolation efficiency under low light or stressful emergence conditions. Deployment should consider isoform-specific expression and coupling with plastid development genes and lipid/assembly factors to avoid photoreactive protochlorophyllide accumulation. Best use is context-specific tuning rather than constitutive overaccumulation.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes light-dependent conversion of protochlorophyllide to chlorophyllide, enabling chlorophyll accumulation, prolamellar body dynamics, chloroplast maturation and photomorphogenesis.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical enzyme assays; purified protein photocatalysis; low-temperature spectroscopy in vivo and in vitro; knockout/complementation in Arabidopsis; structural biology (X-ray/cryo-EM); metabolite/pigment analyses.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Dark-to-light transition; etiolation; visible-light illumination; low light during greening; oxygen context relevant in comparison with DPOR.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly relevant in etiolated seedlings/etioplast-containing tissues; isoform-specific expression during greening and throughout plant life cycle depending on LPORA/LPORB-type genes.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: In plants, strongly associated with dark-grown seedlings and greening tissues; in cyanobacteria/algae also present as chlorophyll biosynthesis enzyme.
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Pchlide a oxygenase; tyrosine aminotransferase; chaperone-like protein of POR1; FLU; CHL27; phycocyanobilin:ferredoxin oxidoreductase; light-harvesting-like proteins; POR-interacting TPR protein
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Phylogenetic analysis of LPOR sequences mentioned; no accession reported in provided text.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review article synthesizing evidence across species. LPOR is described as a master switch linking chlorophyll biosynthesis with plastid membrane remodeling and photosynthetic apparatus assembly. Multiple isoforms exist in angiosperms; lipid interactions and oligomerization are central to function.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Misregulation could increase phototoxic protochlorophyllide accumulation or alter plastid development; effects may depend on lipid environment and isoform composition.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Light-dependent protochlorophyllide oxidoreductase; NADPH-dependent oxidoreductase with Rossmann fold.
Common_Genes.Source_Species: Plants, algae, cyanobacteria, and some anoxygenic phototrophic bacteria
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Chloroplast/etioplast membranes; prolamellar bodies; plastid-associated membrane filaments
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LPOR catalyzes the penultimate chlorophyll-biosynthetic step, reducing protochlorophyllide to chlorophyllide in a light-dependent manner. 2. Its photocatalysis triggers etioplast-to-chloroplast conversion, PLB dispersal and establishment of the photosynthetic apparatus. 3. LPOR oligomerization, lipid interaction and isoform-specific regulation underpin its developmental control over early photomorphogenesis.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Chloroplasts, especially thylakoid/grana membranes of green tissues
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Harvests light for photosynthesis; essential for chloroplast development and plant autotrophic growth.

Common_Genes.Applied_Species: Photosynthetic organisms including non-flowering plants, algae and cyanobacteria
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: DPOR may be useful for engineering chlorophyll synthesis under low-light or dark conditions and oxygen-variable environments, especially in non-angiosperm or microbial systems. In crop angiosperms it is mainly relevant as an evolutionary comparator unless heterologously introduced.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes light-independent protochlorophyllide reduction to chlorophyllide, supporting chlorophyll synthesis under dark/variable conditions.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative enzymology and phylogenetic/physiological evidence summarized in review.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Dark conditions; oxygen sensitivity; variable light and oxygen environments.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Occurs in non-flowering land plants, algae and cyanobacteria; not the operative route in angiosperms.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Associated with organisms capable of dark-operative chlorophyll biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Multisubunit enzyme with three separate subunits
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Older evolutionary route; ATP dependent and oxygen sensitive; closely resembles nitrogenase.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Oxygen sensitivity constrains deployment in oxygenic contexts.
Common_Genes.Source_Species: Anoxygenic photosynthetic bacteria; non-flowering land plants; algae; cyanobacteria
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DPOR provides a light-independent route for protochlorophyllide reduction. 2. It is evolutionarily older than LPOR and oxygen sensitive. 3. Co-occurrence with LPOR may optimize chlorophyll production under variable environmental conditions.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Photosynthetic membranes/chloroplasts or equivalent photosynthetic compartments
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports photosynthesis and adaptation to variable light/oxygen conditions.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Plant (angiosperms; review includes Arabidopsis, barley, cyanobacteria examples)
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Hordeum vulgare; broader Viridiplantae context
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A high-value target for improving seedling establishment, greening efficiency and photosynthetic apparatus formation. Engineering should use isoform-aware or inducible/light-responsive expression and may be stacked with plastid lipid and assembly factors; overaccumulation must avoid Pchlide phototoxicity.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Light-driven reduction of the C17–C18 double bond of protochlorophyllide to produce chlorophyllide using NADPH.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increases chlorophyllide/chlorophyll formation and acts as the decisive biochemical trigger for de-etiolation and chloroplast development.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Purified enzyme photocatalysis; time-resolved and cryogenic spectroscopy; in vivo low-temperature intermediate detection; mutagenesis; structural biology; Arabidopsis knockout replacement with cyanobacterial LPOR.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms the immediate precursor of chlorophyll; penultimate step directly upstream of terminal pigment formation.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Dark-grown etiolation followed by illumination; visible light; low-light greening.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Strong in etiolated seedlings/etioplasts; isoforms differ, with one preferential in etiolated tissues and another maintaining chlorophyll synthesis through the life cycle.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Present in plants, algae, cyanobacteria and some anoxygenic phototrophic bacteria; expression linked to chlorophyll biosynthesis and light/developmental state.
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: FLU; CHL27; phycocyanobilin:ferredoxin oxidoreductase; light-harvesting-like proteins; POR-interacting TPR protein; Pchlide a oxygenase; tyrosine aminotransferase; chaperone-like protein of POR1
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Penultimate step; late pathway control point
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Phylogenetic sequence analysis mentioned; no accession reported.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: LPOR forms substrate-bound oligomers/filaments in prolamellar bodies, interacts with galactolipids, and links metabolism to plastid ultrastructure. Plant and cyanobacterial enzymes differ in proton-transfer dynamics. Some angiosperms carry multiple LPOR isoforms.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Incorrect timing or expression may disturb PLB dynamics, cause Pchlide buildup and photodamage, or alter oxidative stress/vitamin E status.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step/master switch for chlorophyll accumulation and early photomorphogenesis
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Plants, algae, cyanobacteria, some anoxygenic phototrophic bacteria
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Etioplast/chloroplast membranes; prolamellar bodies; plastid-associated filaments
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LPOR catalyzes light-dependent protochlorophyllide reduction to chlorophyllide. 2. This reaction is the decisive trigger for PLB dispersal, chloroplast biogenesis and photomorphogenesis. 3. LPOR structure, oligomerization and lipid interactions integrate catalytic flux with membrane remodeling.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Chloroplast thylakoid and grana membranes of green tissues
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Light capture for photosynthesis and establishment of the photosynthetic apparatus.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Photosynthetic organisms possessing dark-operative chlorophyll synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potentially useful for synthetic biology of chlorophyll production under dark or low-light conditions, especially in microbial or non-angiosperm systems; limited direct crop use due to oxygen sensitivity.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: ATP-dependent reduction of protochlorophyllide to chlorophyllide in the absence of light.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports chlorophyll biosynthesis independently of light, especially under variable environmental conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative biochemical and physiological evidence summarized in review.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms the immediate precursor of chlorophyll via an alternative route to LPOR.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Dark conditions; oxygen-sensitive reaction; variable light/oxygen environments.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Present in non-flowering land plants, algae and cyanobacteria; absent as functional route in angiosperms.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Associated with species maintaining chlorophyll synthesis in darkness.
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Penultimate step; alternative route to LPOR-catalyzed step
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Evolutionarily older than LPOR and related to nitrogenase.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important alternative control point under dark/oxygen-variable conditions; not established as principal switch in angiosperms
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Anoxygenic photosynthetic bacteria; non-flowering land plants; algae; cyanobacteria
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DPOR is the older, light-independent enzyme for protochlorophyllide reduction. 2. It enables chlorophyll synthesis in darkness but is oxygen sensitive. 3. Coexistence with LPOR may optimize chlorophyll production under fluctuating light and oxygen.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful only as an upstream precursor-supply target in pathway balancing; not a paper focal gene.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts 5-aminolevulinic acid-derived intermediates to porphobilinogen/form upstream tetrapyrrole precursor steps (as summarized in pathway overview).
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to precursor supply for chlorophyll and haem synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Listed only in figure/pathway overview; no gene-specific experiments in this paper.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ALAD is part of the common upstream tetrapyrrole pathway to protoporphyrin IX. 2. It supports precursor formation for chlorophyll biosynthesis. 3. No direct mechanistic focus in this review beyond pathway placement.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Upstream precursor-balancing target only; not directly studied here.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes porphobilinogen polymerization/deamination in the common tetrapyrrole pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports formation of upstream tetrapyrrole intermediates for chlorophyll biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PBGD belongs to the shared tetrapyrrole pathway. 2. It contributes upstream intermediates for chlorophyll formation. 3. No gene-specific experimental analysis is provided in this review.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Upstream pathway component; not focal for engineering in this review.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes hydroxymethylbilane to uroporphyrinogen III in the common tetrapyrrole pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports precursor formation for chlorophyll biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. UROS participates in the common upstream tetrapyrrole pathway. 2. It provides intermediates needed for chlorophyll biosynthesis. 3. No direct experimental treatment in this article.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Upstream pathway-balancing target only; not directly analyzed.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Decarboxylates uroporphyrinogen III in the common tetrapyrrole pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports precursor formation before protoporphyrin IX.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. UROD is an upstream enzyme in the shared tetrapyrrole pathway. 2. It contributes to precursor formation for chlorophyll. 3. The paper mentions it only as pathway context.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Context gene for precursor supply; not specifically studied.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes coproporphyrinogen toward protoporphyrinogen/protoporphyrin intermediates in the common tetrapyrrole pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports formation of protoporphyrin IX precursor for chlorophyll branch entry.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CPOX is part of the shared tetrapyrrole trunk pathway. 2. It contributes to precursor formation for chlorophyll. 3. No gene-specific evidence is detailed here.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Context enzyme for balancing precursor flux; not directly studied.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes protoporphyrinogen IX to protoporphyrin IX.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces branch-point intermediate for haem versus chlorophyll synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PPOX forms protoporphyrin IX, the branch-point precursor for chlorophyll biosynthesis. 2. It supports metabolic entry into the Mg branch. 3. It is included only in the pathway overview.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential branch-control target for flux into chlorophyll; not focal in this paper.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Inserts Mg2+ into protoporphyrin IX using ATP to commit flux toward chlorophyll synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Commits tetrapyrrole flux to chlorophyll branch.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Commits precursor pool to target-product branch.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Contrasted with ferrochelatase-mediated Fe insertion into haem branch.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Flux diversion from haem if strongly manipulated.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Mg-chelatase inserts Mg2+ into protoporphyrin IX to begin the chlorophyll branch. 2. This step determines partitioning between haem and chlorophyll synthesis. 3. The review includes it as pathway context rather than direct mechanistic focus.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Haem biosynthesis branch competing with chlorophyll
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Relevant to branch partitioning between haem and chlorophyll; not focal here.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Inserts Fe2+ into protoporphyrin IX to form haem, competing with the Mg-chelatase chlorophyll branch.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Diverts branch-point precursor away from chlorophyll to haem.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because protoporphyrin IX is a branch point between haem and chlorophyll.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Branch competitor, not established in this paper as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Ferrochelatase channels protoporphyrin IX into haem rather than chlorophyll. 2. It defines competition at the tetrapyrrole branch point. 3. The paper uses it as context for LPOR's downstream role.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential auxiliary pathway target due to interaction with LPOR and flux control; direct engineering implications unclear from this review.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Not described in detail here; named as a regulatory/interacting chlorophyll biosynthesis enzyme associated with LPOR and pathway flux control.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Implicated in controlling flux through chlorophyll biosynthesis via interaction with LPOR.
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: CHL27 (Mg-protoporphyrin IX monomethyl ester cyclase associated factor/enzyme designation not detailed here)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because specifically named as LPOR-interacting/regulatory component; catalytic assignment not elaborated in provided text.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CHL27 is named as an LPOR-associated component controlling chlorophyll pathway flux. 2. It likely functions in late chlorophyll biosynthesis. 3. The review does not provide direct catalytic or mutant data for this component.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for fine-tuning chlorophyll-pathway flux and preventing toxic intermediate accumulation. Likely better for modulation than knockout because of its protective role in pathway homeostasis.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Regulates chlorophyll biosynthetic flux through interaction with LPOR and other pathway components.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller of chlorophyll biosynthesis homeostasis, helping prevent overaccumulation of photoreactive intermediates.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Likely homeostatic/feedback-related control of tetrapyrrole flux, as implied by pathway regulation to avoid photoreactive intermediate accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Named as a regulatory protein interacting with LPOR to control flux through the chlorophyll biosynthesis pathway.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Misregulation may permit accumulation of photoreactive tetrapyrrole intermediates and photodamage.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: LPOR-associated chlorophyll biosynthesis module; CHL27-associated flux control
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Acts in an LPOR-associated regulatory complex/module
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FLU is an LPOR-interacting regulatory component in chlorophyll biosynthesis. 2. It contributes to control of pathway flux and prevention of harmful intermediate buildup. 3. Its action is positioned within the broader light-regulated network governing chlorophyll accumulation.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to coordinate chlorophyll synthesis with photosynthetic complex assembly; requires cautious tuning due to assembly-linked effects.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Acts downstream of LPOR in assembly pathway formation of photosynthetic complexes during greening.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Important coordinator linking chlorophyll production to assembly of chlorophyll-binding complexes rather than direct catalytic control.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Assembly/sequestration of chlorophyll into photosynthetic complexes
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Named as a downstream LPOR interactor involved in assembly of photosynthetic complexes.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulation may uncouple pigment synthesis from complex assembly, causing photooxidative stress.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Photosynthetic complex assembly module associated with LPOR
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Not described at transcriptional level; coordinates LPOR-linked complex assembly
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The POR-interacting TPR protein acts downstream of LPOR during photosynthetic apparatus assembly. 2. It links chlorophyll biosynthesis with formation of chlorophyll-binding complexes. 3. It exemplifies how LPOR interfaces with structural development beyond catalysis.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Light-driven LPOR activation and greening state

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Indirect target for coordinating pigment biosynthesis with photoprotection and assembly; use in combination with LPOR engineering may improve safe greening.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Participates downstream of LPOR in formation of photosynthetic complexes and likely photoprotection during chloroplast development.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent facilitators ensuring newly synthesized chlorophyll is safely incorporated during greening.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as downstream proteins interacting with LPOR during assembly of photosynthetic complexes.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Misregulation may increase photooxidative risk during greening.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Photosynthetic complex assembly and chlorophyll-binding module
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Light-harvesting-like proteins are downstream LPOR interactors involved in photosynthetic complex assembly. 2. They help couple chlorophyll production to safe membrane/protein complex formation. 3. Their role underscores the developmental reach of LPOR photocatalysis.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Photosynthetic light capture and energy transfer.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Primarily a signaling target for synchronizing chlorophyll biosynthesis with light cues. Could be used to tune light-responsive greening, but broad pleiotropy is expected.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Controls expression of chlorophyll biosynthesis genes through photoreceptor-mediated light-responsive signaling cascades.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: General regulatory evidence summarized in review; no single-gene experiment described here.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major upstream light-sensing controller of chlorophyll-pathway gene expression.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Feedforward from light perception to chlorophyll biosynthetic gene expression.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Transcriptional control of chlorophyll biosynthesis and photosynthesis gene expression
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as example of photoreceptor-mediated cascade controlling many chlorophyll pathway genes.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad developmental pleiotropy beyond chlorophyll metabolism.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Large co-expression network of chlorophyll biosynthesis genes and downstream photosynthesis genes
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Photoreceptor signaling complexes not detailed here
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates/reprograms light-responsive chlorophyll biosynthesis and photosynthesis genes
Regulation_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Photoreceptor signaling compartments including cytosol/nucleus depending on state
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Phytochrome-mediated signaling regulates chlorophyll biosynthesis gene expression. 2. It links light perception to the establishment of photosynthetic capacity. 3. The review positions it upstream of the LPOR-centered developmental switch.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential upstream target for tuning light-responsive chlorophyll accumulation, though broad pleiotropic effects likely.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Participates in photoreceptor-mediated signaling that regulates chlorophyll biosynthesis genes and downstream photosynthesis genes.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: General regulatory evidence summarized in review; no single-gene experiment described here.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Important upstream light-sensing controller acting with phytochrome.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Feedforward from light perception to biosynthetic gene expression.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Transcriptional control of chlorophyll biosynthesis and photosynthesis gene expression
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as example photoreceptor in light-responsive signaling cascades controlling pathway genes.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad developmental effects beyond nutrient metabolism.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Chlorophyll biosynthesis co-expression network and downstream photosynthesis genes
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Photoreceptor signaling complexes not detailed here
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates/reprograms light-responsive chlorophyll biosynthesis gene expression
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Cryptochrome is part of the photoreceptor signaling network regulating chlorophyll biosynthesis genes. 2. It couples light perception to greening and photosynthetic gene expression. 3. The review cites it as an upstream regulator of the chlorophyll network.
